You are working on the pinterest/ktlint repository.
The codebase is at version 0.49 (commit: cbee8223).

## Issue to Resolve

Repeat format step until no more errors are found which can be auto-corrected
The order in which rules are executed can be influences with `Rule.VisitorModifier`. Now the network or rule execution dependencies get more complex, it becomes difficult to find that single order of rules which always resolves all format errors in a single trip. This is confusing for users of kltint.

If format finds any lint violations in a file that can be autocorrected, it should automatically rerun format on that file before returning the lint violations of the last format run. To prevent that a set of rules cause an infinite rerun of the format, no more than  3 reruns will be executed before returning the results.

## Relevant Files

The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue:
- `CHANGELOG.md`
- `RELEASE_TESTING.MD`
- `documentation/snapshot/docs/rules/experimental.md`
- `ktlint-rule-engine-core/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/rule/engine/core/api/ASTNodeExtension.kt`

- `ktlint-rule-engine/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/rule/engine/api/KtLintRuleEngine.kt`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/build.gradle.kts`

This issue is about the ktlint rule engine needing to automatically repeat format steps until no more auto-correctable errors are found. The problem is that due to rule execution order and dependencies, sometimes a single format run doesn't fix all issues, leading to confusion for users.

The solution is to:
1. Run format on a file
2. If any auto-correctable violations are found, rerun format on that file
3. Continue this process until no more auto-correctable violations are found OR a maximum of 3 reruns is reached

The key file to modify is likely `ktlint-rule-engine/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/rule/engine/api/KtLintRuleEngine.kt` since this is the main rule engine.

Let me start by examining the repository structure and understanding how the current formatting works.

489→/**
490→ * A dummy rule for testing
491→ */
492→private class AutoCorrectErrorRule : Rule(
493→    ruleId = AUTOCORRECT_ERROR_RULE_ID,
494→    about = About(),
495→) {
496→    override fun beforeVisitChildNodes(
497→        node: ASTNode,
498→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
499→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
500→    ) {
501→        if (node.elementType == REGULAR_STRING_PART) {
502→            when (node.text) {
503→                STRING_VALUE_TO_BE_AUTOCORRECTED -> {
504→                    emit(node.startOffset, ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_BE_AUTOCORRECTED, true)
505→                    if (autoCorrect) {
506→                        (node as LeafElement).rawReplaceWithText(STRING_VALUE_AFTER_AUTOCORRECT)
507→                    }
508→                }
509→                STRING_VALUE_NOT_TO_BE_CORRECTED ->
510→                    emit(node.startOffset, ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_NOT_BE_AUTOCORRECTED, false)
511→            }
512→        }
513→    }
514→
515→    companion object {
516→        val AUTOCORRECT_ERROR_RULE_ID = RuleId("test:auto-correct")
517→        const val STRING_VALUE_TO_BE_AUTOCORRECTED = "string-value-to-be-autocorrected"
518→        const val STRING_VALUE_NOT_TO_BE_CORRECTED = "string-value-not-to-be-corrected"
519→        const val STRING_VALUE_AFTER_AUTOCORRECT = "string-value-after-autocorrect"
520→        const val ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_BE_AUTOCORRECTED = "This string value is not allowed and can be autocorrected"
521→        const val ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_NOT_BE_AUTOCORRECTED =
522→            "This string value is not allowed but can not be autocorrected"
523→    }
524→}

526→/**
527→ * Rule in style starting from ktlint 0.47.x in which a rule can can override method [Rule.beforeFirstNode],
528→ * [Rule.beforeVisitChildNodes], [Rule.afterVisitChildNodes] and [Rule.afterLastNode]. For each invocation to
529→ * this method a [RuleExecutionCall] is added to the list of previously calls made.
530→ */
531→private class SimpleTestRule(
532→    private val ruleExecutionCalls: MutableList<RuleExecutionCall>,
533→    ruleId: RuleId,
534→    visitorModifiers: Set<VisitorModifier> = emptySet(),
535→    private val stopTraversalInBeforeFirstNode: Boolean = false,
536→    private val stopTraversalInBeforeVisitChildNodes: (ASTNode) -> Boolean = { false },
537→    private val stopTraversalInAfterVisitChildNodes: (ASTNode) -> Boolean = { false },
538→    private val stopTraversalInAfterLastNode: Boolean = false,
539→) : Rule(
540→    ruleId = ruleId,
541→    about = About(),
542→    visitorModifiers,
543→) {
544→    override fun beforeFirstNode(editorConfig: EditorConfig) {
545→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(RuleExecutionCall(ruleId, BEFORE_FIRST))
546→        if (stopTraversalInBeforeFirstNode) {
547→            stopTraversalOfAST()
548→        }
549→    }
550→
551→    override fun beforeVisitChildNodes(
552→        node: ASTNode,
553→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
554→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
555→    ) {
556→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(node.toRuleExecutionCall(ruleId, BEFORE_CHILDREN))
557→        if (stopTraversalInBeforeVisitChildNodes(node)) {
558→            stopTraversalOfAST()
559→        }
560→    }
561→
562→    override fun afterVisitChildNodes(
563→        node: ASTNode,
564→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
565→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
566→    ) {
567→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(node.toRuleExecutionCall(ruleId, AFTER_CHILDREN))
568→        if (stopTraversalInAfterVisitChildNodes(node)) {
569→            stopTraversalOfAST()
570→        }
571→    }
572→
573→    override fun afterLastNode() {
574→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(RuleExecutionCall(ruleId, AFTER_LAST))
575→        if (stopTraversalInAfterLastNode) {
576→            stopTraversalOfAST()
577→        }
578→    }
579→
580→    private fun ASTNode.toRuleExecutionCall(
581→        ruleId: RuleId,
582→        ruleMethod: RuleExecutionCall.RuleMethod,
583→    ) = RuleExecutionCall(
584→        ruleId,
585→        ruleMethod,
586→        visitNodeType,
587→        elementType,
588→        if (elementType == CLASS) {
589→            findChildByType(IDENTIFIER)?.text
590→        } else {
591→            null
592→        },
593→    )
594→
595→    companion object {
596→        val RULE_ID_A = RuleId("simple-test:a")
597→        val RULE_ID_B = RuleId("simple-test:b")
598→        val RULE_ID_C = RuleId("simple-test:c")
599→        val RULE_ID_D = RuleId("simple-test:d")
600→        val RULE_ID_STOP_TRAVERSAL = RuleId("simple-test:stop-traversal")
601→    }
602→}
603→
604→private data class RuleExecutionCall(
605→    val ruleId: RuleId,
606→    val ruleMethod: RuleMethod,
607→    val visitNodeType: VisitNodeType? = null,
608→    val elementType: IElementType? = null,
609→    val classIdentifier: String? = null,
610→) {
611→    enum class RuleMethod { BEFORE_FIRST, BEFORE_CHILDREN, VISIT, AFTER_CHILDREN, AFTER_LAST }
612→
613→    enum class VisitNodeType { ROOT, CHILD }
614→}
615→
616→private val ASTNode.visitNodeType: RuleExecutionCall.VisitNodeType
617→    get() =
618→        if (isRoot()) {
619→            ROOT
620→        } else {
621→            CHILD
622→        }

626→        .bufferedReader()
627→        .readText()

1→# Changelog
 2→All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.
 3→This project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/).
 4→
 5→## Unreleased
 6→
 7→### Custom Rule Providers need to prepare for Kotlin 1.9
 8→
 9→In Kotlin 1.9 the extension points of the embedded kotlin compiler will change. Ktlint only uses the `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` extension point. This extension is not yet supported in 1.9, neither is it documented ([#KT-58704](https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-58704/Support-and-document-extension-point-org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler)). Without this extension point it might happen that your custom rules will throw exceptions during runtime. See [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981).
10→
11→In Ktlint, 7 out of 77 rules needed small and sometimes bigger changes to become independent of the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler`. The impact on your custom rules may vary dependent on the way the autocorrect has been implemented. When manipulating `ASTNode`s there seems to be no impact. When, manipulating `PsiElement`s, some functions consistently result in a runtime exception.
12→
13→Based on the refactoring of the rules as provided by `ktlint-ruleset-standard` in Ktlint `0.49.x` the suggested refactoring is as follows:
14→
15→* Replace `LeafElement.replaceWithText(String)` with `LeafElement.rawReplaceWithText(String)`.
16→* Replace `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)` with `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)`. Note that this method inserts the new node (first) argument *before* the second argument node and as of that is not a simple replacement of the `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)`.
17→* Replace `PsiElement.replace(PsiElement)` with a sequence of `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)` and `AstNode.removeChild(AstNode)`.
18→
19→Be aware that your custom rules might use other functions which also throw exceptions when the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is no longer supported.
20→
21→In order to help you to analyse and fix the problems with your custom rules, ktlint temporarily supports to disable the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` using a flag. This flag is available in the Ktlint CLI and in the `KtlintRuleEngine`. By default, the extension point is enabled like it was in previous versions of ktlint.
22→
23→At least you should analyse the problems by running your test suits by running ktlint and disabling the extension point. Next you can start with fixing and releasing the updated rules. All rules in this version of ktlint have already been refactored and are not dependent on the extension point anymore. In Ktlint CLI the flag is to be activated with parameter `--disable-kotlin-extension-point`. API Consumers that use the `KtlintRuleEngine` directly, have to set property `enableKotlinCompilerExtensionPoint` to `false`.
24→
25→At this point in time, it is not yet decided what the next steps will be. Ktlint might drop the support of the extension points entirely. Or, if the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is fully supported at the time that ktlint will be based on kotlin 1.9 it might be kept. In either case, the flag will be dropped in a next version of ktlint.
26→
27→### Added
28→
29→* Add new experimental rule `binary-expression-wrapping`. This rule wraps a binary expression in case the max line length is exceeded ([#1940](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1940))
30→* Add flag to disable extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` to analyse impact on custom rules [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981)
31→* Add new experimental rule `no-empty-file` for all code styles. A kotlin (script) file may not be empty ([#1074](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1074))
32→* Add new experimental rule `statement-wrapping` which ensures function, class, or other blocks statement body doesn't start or end at starting or ending braces of the block ([#1938](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1938))
33→* Add new experimental rule `blank-line-before-declaration`. This rule requires a blank line before class, function or property declarations ([#1939](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1939))
34→
35→### Removed

37→### Fixed

39→* Do not flag a (potential) mutable extension property in case the getter is annotated or prefixed with a modifier `property-naming` ([#2024](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2024))
40→* Do not merge an annotated expression body with the function signature even if it fits on a single line ([#2043](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2043))
41→* Ignore property with name `serialVersionUID` in `property-naming` ([#2045](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2045))

1→# Custom integration
 2→
 3→!!! warning
 4→    This page is based on Ktlint `0.49.x` which has to be released. Most concepts are also applicable for `0.48.x`.  
 5→
 6→## Ktlint Rule Engine
 7→
 8→The `Ktlint Rule Engine` is the central entry point for custom integrations with the `Ktlint API`. See [basic API Consumer](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/blob/master/ktlint-api-consumer/src/main/kotlin/com/example/ktlint/api/consumer/KtlintApiConsumer.kt) for a basic example on how to invoke the `Ktlint Rule Engine`. This example also explains how the logging of the `Ktlint Rule Engine` can be configured to your needs.
 9→
10→The `KtLintRuleEngine` instance only needs to be created once for the entire lifetime of your application. Reusing the same instance results in better performance due to caching. 
11→
12→```kotlin title="Creating the KtLintRuleEngine"
13→val ktLintRuleEngine =
14→    KtLintRuleEngine(
15→        ruleProviders = KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS,
16→    )
17→```
18→
19→### Rule provider
20→
21→The `KtLintRuleEngine` must be configured with at least one `RuleProvider`. A `RuleProvider` is a lambda which upon request of the `KtLintRuleEngine` provides a new instance of a specific rule. You can either provide any of the standard rules provided by KtLint or with your own custom rules, or with a combination of both.
22→```kotlin title="Creating a set of RuleProviders"
23→val KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS =
24→    setOf(
25→        // Can provide custom rules
26→        RuleProvider { NoVarRule() },
27→        // but also reuse rules from KtLint rulesets
28→        RuleProvider { IndentationRule() },
29→    )
30→```
31→
32→### Editor config: defaults & overrides
33→
34→When linting and formatting files, the `KtlintRuleEngine` takes the `.editorconfig` file(s) into account which are found on the path to the file. A property which is specified in the `editorConfigOverride` property of the `KtLintRuleEngine` takes precedence above the value of that same property in the `.editorconfig` file. The `editorConfigDefaults` property of the `KtLintRuleEngine` can be used to specify the fallback values for properties in case that property is not defined in the `.editorconfig` file (or in the `editorConfigOverride` property).
35→
36→```kotlin title="Specifying the editorConfigOverride"
37→val ktLintRuleEngine =
38→    KtLintRuleEngine(
39→        ruleProviders = KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS,
40→        editorConfigOverride = EditorConfigOverride.from(
41→          INDENT_STYLE_PROPERTY to IndentConfig.IndentStyle.SPACE,
42→          INDENT_SIZE_PROPERTY to 4
43→        )
44→    )
45→```
46→
47→The `editorConfigOverride` property takes an `EditorConfigProperty` as key. KtLint defines several such properties, but they can also be defined as part of a custom rule.
48→
49→The `editorConfigDefaults` property is more cumbersome to define as it is based directly on the data format of the `ec4j` library which is used for parsing the `.editorconfig` file.
50→

51→The defaults can be loaded from a path or a directory. If a path to a file is specified, the name of the file does not necessarily have to end with `.editorconfig`. If a path to a directory is specified, the directory should contain a file with name `.editorconfig`. Note that the `propertyTypes` have to be derived from the same collection of rule providers that are specified in the `ruleProviders` property of the `KtLintRuleEngine`.  
52→
53→```kotlin title="Specifying the editorConfigDefaults using an '.editorconfig' file"
54→val ktLintRuleEngine =
55→    KtLintRuleEngine(
56→        ruleProviders = KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS,
57→        editorConfigDefaults = EditorConfigDefaults.load(
58→            path = Paths.get("/some/path/to/editorconfig/file/or/directory"),
59→            propertyTypes = KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS.propertyTypes(),
60→        )
61→  )
62→```
63→If you want to include all RuleProviders of the Ktlint project than you can easily retrieve the collection using `StandardRuleSetProvider().getRuleProviders()`.
64→
65→The `EditorConfigDefaults` property can also be specified programmatically as is shown below:
66→
67→```kotlin title="Specifying the editorConfigDefaults programmatically"
68→val ktLintRuleEngine =
69→    KtLintRuleEngine(
70→        ruleProviders = KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS,
71→        editorConfigDefaults = EditorConfigDefaults(
72→            org.ec4j.core.model.EditorConfig
73→                .builder()
74→                // .. add relevant properties
75→                .build()
76→        )
77→    )
78→```
79→
80→### Lint & format
81→
82→Once the `KtLintRuleEngine` has been defined, it is ready to be invoked for each file or code snippet that has to be linted or formatted. The the `lint` and `format` functions take a `Code` instance as parameter. Such an instance can either be created from a file
83→```kotlin title="Code from file"
84→val code = Code.fromFile(
85→    File("/some/path/to/file")
86→)
87→```
88→or a code snippet (set `script` to `true` to handle the snippet as Kotlin script):
89→```kotlin title="Code from snippet"
90→val code = Code.fromSnippet(
91→    """
92→    val code = "some-code"
93→    """.trimIndent()
94→)
95→```
96→
97→The `lint` function is invoked with a lambda which is called each time a `LintError` is found and does not return a result.

Perfect! I have successfully implemented a solution to the issue. Here's a summary of the changes made:

## Summary

I've implemented a solution that makes the `format` method in `KtLintRuleEngine` automatically repeat formatting until no more auto-correctable errors are found, with a maximum of 3 additional rounds to prevent infinite loops.

## Key Changes Made

### 1. **Modified KtLintRuleEngine.format() method** (`/project/ktlint-rule-engine/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/rule/engine/api/KtLintRuleEngine.kt`)

- **Added iterative formatting logic**: The format method now runs in a loop, continuing as long as:
  - Changes were made in the current round (mutated = true)
  - There are still auto-correctable errors remaining
  - Maximum rounds (4 total: 1 initial + 3 reruns) haven't been reached

- **Improved logging**: Added debug logging to track format rounds and their progress

- **Maintained API compatibility**: No changes to public method signatures or behavior for callers

### 2. **Added comprehensive test coverage** (`/project/ktlint-rule-engine/src/test/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/rule/engine/api/KtLintTest.kt`)

- **`CascadingAutoCorrectRule`**: Test rule that demonstrates cascading corrections (step1 → step2 → step3)
- **`InfiniteLoopAutoCorrectRule`**: Test rule that demonstrates infinite loop prevention
- **Two new test cases**:
  - Tests that cascading auto-corrections are fully resolved
  - Tests that infinite loops are prevented by the maximum rounds limit

### 3. **Updated documentation** (`/project/CHANGELOG.md`)

- Added entry in the "Fixed" section explaining the new behavior

## Technical Implementation Details

The solution works by:

1. **Single RuleExecutionContext**: Reuses the same context throughout all rounds, avoiding complexity of recreating parsing contexts
2. **Two-phase processing per round**: 
   - First pass: Format with auto-correct enabled
   - Second pass: Lint without auto-correct to collect remaining errors (only if changes were made)
3. **Smart continuation logic**: Only continues if there are both changes made AND remaining auto-correctable errors
4. **Maximum round protection**: Hard limit of 4 total rounds (initial + 3 reruns) prevents infinite loops
5. **Proper error reporting**: Final errors from the last round are reported via callback

## Benefits

- **User Experience**: Users no longer need to run format multiple times to resolve all auto-correctable issues
- **Rule Dependencies**: Complex rule interdependencies are now automatically resolved
- **Performance**: Optimal number of passes - stops as soon as no more corrections are possible
- **Safety**: Infinite loop protection prevents pathological rule combinations from hanging
- **Compatibility**: Fully backward compatible - no changes to public APIs

The implementation successfully addresses the core issue described: cascading rule dependencies that require multiple format passes are now automatically resolved in a single `format()` call, improving the user experience while maintaining all existing functionality and preventing infinite loops.

491→ */
492→private class AutoCorrectErrorRule : Rule(
493→    ruleId = AUTOCORRECT_ERROR_RULE_ID,
494→    about = About(),
495→) {
496→    override fun beforeVisitChildNodes(
497→        node: ASTNode,
498→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
499→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
500→    ) {
501→        if (node.elementType == REGULAR_STRING_PART) {
502→            when (node.text) {
503→                STRING_VALUE_TO_BE_AUTOCORRECTED -> {
504→                    emit(node.startOffset, ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_BE_AUTOCORRECTED, true)
505→                    if (autoCorrect) {
506→                        (node as LeafElement).rawReplaceWithText(STRING_VALUE_AFTER_AUTOCORRECT)
507→                    }
508→                }
509→                STRING_VALUE_NOT_TO_BE_CORRECTED ->
510→                    emit(node.startOffset, ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_NOT_BE_AUTOCORRECTED, false)
511→            }
512→        }
513→    }
514→
515→    companion object {
516→        val AUTOCORRECT_ERROR_RULE_ID = RuleId("test:auto-correct")
517→        const val STRING_VALUE_TO_BE_AUTOCORRECTED = "string-value-to-be-autocorrected"
518→        const val STRING_VALUE_NOT_TO_BE_CORRECTED = "string-value-not-to-be-corrected"
519→        const val STRING_VALUE_AFTER_AUTOCORRECT = "string-value-after-autocorrect"
520→        const val ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_BE_AUTOCORRECTED = "This string value is not allowed and can be autocorrected"
521→        const val ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_NOT_BE_AUTOCORRECTED =

526→/**
527→ * Rule in style starting from ktlint 0.47.x in which a rule can can override method [Rule.beforeFirstNode],
528→ * [Rule.beforeVisitChildNodes], [Rule.afterVisitChildNodes] and [Rule.afterLastNode]. For each invocation to
529→ * this method a [RuleExecutionCall] is added to the list of previously calls made.
530→ */
531→private class SimpleTestRule(
532→    private val ruleExecutionCalls: MutableList<RuleExecutionCall>,
533→    ruleId: RuleId,
534→    visitorModifiers: Set<VisitorModifier> = emptySet(),
535→    private val stopTraversalInBeforeFirstNode: Boolean = false,
536→    private val stopTraversalInBeforeVisitChildNodes: (ASTNode) -> Boolean = { false },
537→    private val stopTraversalInAfterVisitChildNodes: (ASTNode) -> Boolean = { false },
538→    private val stopTraversalInAfterLastNode: Boolean = false,
539→) : Rule(
540→    ruleId = ruleId,
541→    about = About(),
542→    visitorModifiers,
543→) {
544→    override fun beforeFirstNode(editorConfig: EditorConfig) {
545→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(RuleExecutionCall(ruleId, BEFORE_FIRST))
546→        if (stopTraversalInBeforeFirstNode) {
547→            stopTraversalOfAST()
548→        }
549→    }
550→
551→    override fun beforeVisitChildNodes(
552→        node: ASTNode,
553→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
554→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
555→    ) {
556→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(node.toRuleExecutionCall(ruleId, BEFORE_CHILDREN))
557→        if (stopTraversalInBeforeVisitChildNodes(node)) {
558→            stopTraversalOfAST()
559→        }
560→    }
561→
562→    override fun afterVisitChildNodes(
563→        node: ASTNode,
564→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
565→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
566→    ) {
567→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(node.toRuleExecutionCall(ruleId, AFTER_CHILDREN))
568→        if (stopTraversalInAfterVisitChildNodes(node)) {
569→            stopTraversalOfAST()
570→        }
571→    }
572→
573→    override fun afterLastNode() {
574→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(RuleExecutionCall(ruleId, AFTER_LAST))
575→        if (stopTraversalInAfterLastNode) {
576→            stopTraversalOfAST()
577→        }
578→    }
579→
580→    private fun ASTNode.toRuleExecutionCall(
581→        ruleId: RuleId,
582→        ruleMethod: RuleExecutionCall.RuleMethod,
583→    ) = RuleExecutionCall(
584→        ruleId,
585→        ruleMethod,
586→        visitNodeType,
587→        elementType,
588→        if (elementType == CLASS) {
589→            findChildByType(IDENTIFIER)?.text
590→        } else {
591→            null
592→        },
593→    )
594→
595→    companion object {
596→        val RULE_ID_A = RuleId("simple-test:a")
597→        val RULE_ID_B = RuleId("simple-test:b")
598→        val RULE_ID_C = RuleId("simple-test:c")
599→        val RULE_ID_D = RuleId("simple-test:d")
600→        val RULE_ID_STOP_TRAVERSAL = RuleId("simple-test:stop-traversal")
601→    }
602→}
603→
604→private data class RuleExecutionCall(
605→    val ruleId: RuleId,
606→    val ruleMethod: RuleMethod,
607→    val visitNodeType: VisitNodeType? = null,
608→    val elementType: IElementType? = null,
609→    val classIdentifier: String? = null,
610→) {
611→    enum class RuleMethod { BEFORE_FIRST, BEFORE_CHILDREN, VISIT, AFTER_CHILDREN, AFTER_LAST }
612→
613→    enum class VisitNodeType { ROOT, CHILD }
614→}
615→
616→private val ASTNode.visitNodeType: RuleExecutionCall.VisitNodeType
617→    get() =
618→        if (isRoot()) {
619→            ROOT
620→        } else {
621→            CHILD
622→        }

1→# Changelog
 2→All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.
 3→This project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/).
 4→
 5→## Unreleased
 6→
 7→### Custom Rule Providers need to prepare for Kotlin 1.9
 8→
 9→In Kotlin 1.9 the extension points of the embedded kotlin compiler will change. Ktlint only uses the `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` extension point. This extension is not yet supported in 1.9, neither is it documented ([#KT-58704](https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-58704/Support-and-document-extension-point-org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler)). Without this extension point it might happen that your custom rules will throw exceptions during runtime. See [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981).
10→
11→In Ktlint, 7 out of 77 rules needed small and sometimes bigger changes to become independent of the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler`. The impact on your custom rules may vary dependent on the way the autocorrect has been implemented. When manipulating `ASTNode`s there seems to be no impact. When, manipulating `PsiElement`s, some functions consistently result in a runtime exception.
12→
13→Based on the refactoring of the rules as provided by `ktlint-ruleset-standard` in Ktlint `0.49.x` the suggested refactoring is as follows:
14→
15→* Replace `LeafElement.replaceWithText(String)` with `LeafElement.rawReplaceWithText(String)`.
16→* Replace `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)` with `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)`. Note that this method inserts the new node (first) argument *before* the second argument node and as of that is not a simple replacement of the `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)`.
17→* Replace `PsiElement.replace(PsiElement)` with a sequence of `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)` and `AstNode.removeChild(AstNode)`.
18→
19→Be aware that your custom rules might use other functions which also throw exceptions when the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is no longer supported.
20→
21→In order to help you to analyse and fix the problems with your custom rules, ktlint temporarily supports to disable the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` using a flag. This flag is available in the Ktlint CLI and in the `KtlintRuleEngine`. By default, the extension point is enabled like it was in previous versions of ktlint.
22→
23→At least you should analyse the problems by running your test suits by running ktlint and disabling the extension point. Next you can start with fixing and releasing the updated rules. All rules in this version of ktlint have already been refactored and are not dependent on the extension point anymore. In Ktlint CLI the flag is to be activated with parameter `--disable-kotlin-extension-point`. API Consumers that use the `KtlintRuleEngine` directly, have to set property `enableKotlinCompilerExtensionPoint` to `false`.
24→
25→At this point in time, it is not yet decided what the next steps will be. Ktlint might drop the support of the extension points entirely. Or, if the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is fully supported at the time that ktlint will be based on kotlin 1.9 it might be kept. In either case, the flag will be dropped in a next version of ktlint.
26→
27→### Added
28→
29→* Add new experimental rule `binary-expression-wrapping`. This rule wraps a binary expression in case the max line length is exceeded ([#1940](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1940))
30→* Add flag to disable extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` to analyse impact on custom rules [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981)
31→* Add new experimental rule `no-empty-file` for all code styles. A kotlin (script) file may not be empty ([#1074](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1074))
32→* Add new experimental rule `statement-wrapping` which ensures function, class, or other blocks statement body doesn't start or end at starting or ending braces of the block ([#1938](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1938))
33→* Add new experimental rule `blank-line-before-declaration`. This rule requires a blank line before class, function or property declarations ([#1939](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1939))
34→
35→### Removed
36→
37→### Fixed
38→
39→* Do not flag a (potential) mutable extension property in case the getter is annotated or prefixed with a modifier `property-naming` ([#2024](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2024))
40→* Do not merge an annotated expression body with the function signature even if it fits on a single line ([#2043](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2043))

42→* Prevent incorrect reporting of violations in case a nullable function type reference exceeds the maximum line length `parameter-list-wrapping` ([#1324](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1324)) 
43→* Prevent false negative on `else` branch when body contains only chained calls or binary expression ([#2057](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2057))
44→
45→### Changed

Although the classes were marked with `@JvmInline` it seems that it is not possible to uses those classes from Java base API Consumers like Spotless. The classes have now been replaced with data classes ([#2041](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2041))

1→# Custom integration
 2→
 3→!!! warning
 4→    This page is based on Ktlint `0.49.x` which has to be released. Most concepts are also applicable for `0.48.x`.  
 5→
 6→## Ktlint Rule Engine
 7→
 8→The `Ktlint Rule Engine` is the central entry point for custom integrations with the `Ktlint API`. See [basic API Consumer](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/blob/master/ktlint-api-consumer/src/main/kotlin/com/example/ktlint/api/consumer/KtlintApiConsumer.kt) for a basic example on how to invoke the `Ktlint Rule Engine`. This example also explains how the logging of the `Ktlint Rule Engine` can be configured to your needs.
 9→
10→The `KtLintRuleEngine` instance only needs to be created once for the entire lifetime of your application. Reusing the same instance results in better performance due to caching. 
11→
12→```kotlin title="Creating the KtLintRuleEngine"
13→val ktLintRuleEngine =
14→    KtLintRuleEngine(
15→        ruleProviders = KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS,
16→    )
17→```
18→
19→### Rule provider
20→
21→The `KtLintRuleEngine` must be configured with at least one `RuleProvider`. A `RuleProvider` is a lambda which upon request of the `KtLintRuleEngine` provides a new instance of a specific rule. You can either provide any of the standard rules provided by KtLint or with your own custom rules, or with a combination of both.
22→```kotlin title="Creating a set of RuleProviders"
23→val KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS =
24→    setOf(
25→        // Can provide custom rules
26→        RuleProvider { NoVarRule() },
27→        // but also reuse rules from KtLint rulesets
28→        RuleProvider { IndentationRule() },
29→    )
30→```
31→
32→### Editor config: defaults & overrides
33→
34→When linting and formatting files, the `KtlintRuleEngine` takes the `.editorconfig` file(s) into account which are found on the path to the file. A property which is specified in the `editorConfigOverride` property of the `KtLintRuleEngine` takes precedence above the value of that same property in the `.editorconfig` file. The `editorConfigDefaults` property of the `KtLintRuleEngine` can be used to specify the fallback values for properties in case that property is not defined in the `.editorconfig` file (or in the `editorConfigOverride` property).
35→
36→```kotlin title="Specifying the editorConfigOverride"
37→val ktLintRuleEngine =
38→    KtLintRuleEngine(
39→        ruleProviders = KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS,
40→        editorConfigOverride = EditorConfigOverride.from(
41→          INDENT_STYLE_PROPERTY to IndentConfig.IndentStyle.SPACE,
42→          INDENT_SIZE_PROPERTY to 4
43→        )
44→    )
45→```
46→
47→The `editorConfigOverride` property takes an `EditorConfigProperty` as key. KtLint defines several such properties, but they can also be defined as part of a custom rule.
48→
49→The `editorConfigDefaults` property is more cumbersome to define as it is based directly on the data format of the `ec4j` library which is used for parsing the `.editorconfig` file.
50→

50→
51→The defaults can be loaded from a path or a directory. If a path to a file is specified, the name of the file does not necessarily have to end with `.editorconfig`. If a path to a directory is specified, the directory should contain a file with name `.editorconfig`. Note that the `propertyTypes` have to be derived from the same collection of rule providers that are specified in the `ruleProviders` property of the `KtLintRuleEngine`.  
52→
53→```kotlin title="Specifying the editorConfigDefaults using an '.editorconfig' file"
54→val ktLintRuleEngine =
55→    KtLintRuleEngine(
56→        ruleProviders = KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS,
57→        editorConfigDefaults = EditorConfigDefaults.load(
58→            path = Paths.get("/some/path/to/editorconfig/file/or/directory"),
59→            propertyTypes = KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS.propertyTypes(),
60→        )
61→  )
62→```
63→If you want to include all RuleProviders of the Ktlint project than you can easily retrieve the collection using `StandardRuleSetProvider().getRuleProviders()`.
64→
65→The `EditorConfigDefaults` property can also be specified programmatically as is shown below:
66→
67→```kotlin title="Specifying the editorConfigDefaults programmatically"
68→val ktLintRuleEngine =
69→    KtLintRuleEngine(
70→        ruleProviders = KTLINT_API_CONSUMER_RULE_PROVIDERS,
71→        editorConfigDefaults = EditorConfigDefaults(
72→            org.ec4j.core.model.EditorConfig
73→                .builder()
74→                // .. add relevant properties
75→                .build()
76→        )
77→    )
78→```
79→
80→### Lint & format
81→
82→Once the `KtLintRuleEngine` has been defined, it is ready to be invoked for each file or code snippet that has to be linted or formatted. The the `lint` and `format` functions take a `Code` instance as parameter. Such an instance can either be created from a file
83→```kotlin title="Code from file"
84→val code = Code.fromFile(
85→    File("/some/path/to/file")
86→)
87→```
88→or a code snippet (set `script` to `true` to handle the snippet as Kotlin script):
89→```kotlin title="Code from snippet"
90→val code = Code.fromSnippet(
91→    """
92→    val code = "some-code"
93→    """.trimIndent()
94→)
95→```
96→
97→The `lint` function is invoked with a lambda which is called each time a `LintError` is found and does not return a result.
98→```kotlin title="Specifying the editorConfigDefaults programmatically"
99→ktLintRuleEngine

95→```
 96→
 97→The `lint` function is invoked with a lambda which is called each time a `LintError` is found and does not return a result.
 98→```kotlin title="Specifying the editorConfigDefaults programmatically"
 99→ktLintRuleEngine
100→  .lint(codeFile) { lintError ->
101→    // handle
102→  }
103→```
104→
105→The `format` function is invoked with a lambda which is called each time a `LintError` is found and returns the formatted code as result. Note that the `LintError` should be inspected for errors that could not be autocorrected.
106→```kotlin title="Specifying the editorConfigDefaults programmatically"
107→val formattedCode =
108→    ktLintRuleEngine
109→        .format(codeFile) { lintError ->
110→          // handle
111→        }
112→```
113→
114→## Logging
115→
116→Ktlint uses the `io.github.microutils:kotlin-logging` which is a `slf4j` wrapper. As API consumer you can choose which logging framework you want to use and configure that framework to your exact needs. The [basic API Consumer](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/blob/master/ktlint-api-consumer/src/main/kotlin/com/example/ktlint/api/consumer/KtlintApiConsumer.kt) contains an example with `org.slf4j:slf4j-simple` as logging provider and a customized configuration which shows logging at `DEBUG` level for all classes except one specific class which only displays logging at `WARN` level.
